steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a punctuation question testing how to introduce an elaboration. After “obligations,” the sentence explains what those obligations involve — mutual defense among all NATO members. The decisive clue is that what follows is a full independent clause that elaborates on the noun “obligations”; a colon is the correct mark to introduce a clause that explains or illustrates what precedes it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘obligations; the’: a semicolon joins two independent clauses of equal weight, but here the second clause is meant to explain “obligations,” not stand as a parallel statement — a colon better signals that explanatory relationship. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘obligations. The’: a period creates two separate sentences, severing the explanatory link between “obligations” and the clause that defines what those obligations entail. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘obligations, while the’: a comma alone is too weak to introduce the substantial elaboration that follows, and “while” implies a temporal or concessive relationship that is not present here. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘obligations: while the’: a colon correctly introduces the explanation of what those obligations mean — that all members, including Spain, are mutually bound to defend one another — and “while” within the elaboration correctly sets up the bilateral comparison. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
